memes.label: A Change in the Cabinet
memes.description: A political novel by Hilaire Belloc, published in 1909, which Belloc himself discusses in The Cruise of the Nona as an example of literary realism versus conventional expectation.

memes.label: Agadir Crisis
memes.description: The 1911 international crisis triggered by the German gunboat Panther's arrival at Agadir, Morocco — referenced by Belloc as a pivotal moment in the Anglo-French alignment that preceded the Great War.

memes.label: Alice in Wonderland
memes.description: Lewis Carroll's classic children's book, discussed by Belloc as an example of literary fame rooted in a particular social moment rather than in permanent merit.

memes.label: American Civil War
memes.description: The 1861–1865 internecine conflict in the United States, referenced by Belloc in his critique of the American War of Independence myth as part of his broader analysis of historical mythology and Anglo-American relations.

memes.label: Anvil Point
memes.description: A headland on the Dorset coast near Swanage, marking for Belloc the western boundary of his "home seas" — the familiar stretch of Channel between the Anvil and the South Foreland.

memes.label: Battle of Sedgemoor
memes.description: The 1685 battle fought near Bridgwater in Somerset, the last pitched battle on English soil, referenced by Belloc as the final act of the Monmouth Rebellion and dismissed as not a true battle but a skirmish between untrained forces.

memes.label: Channel Pilot
memes.description: The Admiralty sailing directions for the English Channel, cited by Belloc as an authoritative nautical text praised for its exact, untheorising truthfulness.

memes.label: charles-kingsley
memes.description: Victorian novelist and clergyman, author of *Westward Ho!*, discussed by Belloc for his imprint on the Devon coast and his sharp critical judgment of verse.

memes.label: chichester-harbour
memes.description: West Sussex tidal harbour, also encompassing Bosham, noted for its tide-dependent entry and its place in Saxon history — where Alfred's men hanged captured Vikings.

memes.label: concept-index
memes.description: Complete index of all concept entries in The Cruise of the Nona wiki, dynamically generated from page metadata.

memes.label: A Crystal Age
memes.description: An early utopian novel by W. H. Hudson, discovered by Belloc in a bookshop and praised as a neglected masterpiece that never found the audience it deserved.

memes.label: Dover
memes.description: The great Channel port of Kent, treated by Belloc with characteristic scepticism toward its engineered harbour, yet acknowledged as a historic gateway between Britain and the Continent.

memes.label: event-index
memes.description: Complete index of all event entries in The Cruise of the Nona wiki, dynamically generated from page metadata.

memes.label: featured-pages
memes.description: Index of featured-quality wiki entries, ranked by cross-reference count — the most interconnected and thoroughly developed pages in the wiki.

memes.label: Fog
memes.description: Fog as a treacherous and transformative presence in Belloc's coastal navigation — obscuring landmarks, amplifying isolation, and turning familiar waters into a realm of uncertainty.

memes.label: Fowey
memes.description: A harbour town on the south coast of Cornwall, celebrated by Belloc as the westernmost of the great south-coast harbours.

memes.label: The Gospels
memes.description: The four canonical Gospels of the New Testament, frequently cited by Belloc in defence of their apostolic authority against academic scepticism.

memes.label: hilaire-belloc
memes.description: Author of The Cruise of the Nona — French-born British writer, poet, historian, and sailor who recounts a voyage from Holyhead to the Wash with reflections on politics, religion, history, and literature.

memes.label: Holy Writ
memes.description: A term used by Belloc in The Cruise of the Nona to refer to the Bible, in a passage reflecting on the biblical counsel to tolerate fools — which Belloc wryly concludes is "a counsel of perfection ... generally to be avoided."

memes.label: House of Commons
memes.description: The lower house of the British Parliament — an institution Belloc knew intimately as a Member of Parliament and came to regard with deepening disillusionment, documenting its decay across three decades of personal observation.

memes.label: Hungarian Soviet Republic
memes.description: The short-lived 1919 communist regime in Hungary, referenced by Belloc as a prime example of the credulity of the Western governing class, who briefly believed the Jewish Communist revolution in Hungary would succeed.

memes.label: If Winter Comes
memes.description: A bestselling novel by A. S. M. Hutchinson (1921), mentioned by Belloc as an example of the capriciousness of the literary marketplace — a book that sold "by the stack, and by the ton."

memes.label: Ireland
memes.description: Island nation to the west of Britain, a central and recurring theme in Belloc's meditations on history, faith, and the failure of English policy.

memes.label: jack-sound
memes.description: Treacherous tidal passage between Skomer Island and the Welsh mainland, one of the most challenging navigational hazards Belloc faced on the Welsh coast.

memes.label: James Lowther
memes.description: British politician (1855–1949), Speaker of the House of Commons (1905–1921), whom Belloc observed at close quarters during his own parliamentary career and regarded as a genius of the chair.

memes.label: John Henry Newman
memes.description: English theologian, poet, and Catholic convert (1801–1890), canonised in 2019, whose prose and conversion Belloc references as touchstones of Catholic intellectual life.

memes.label: John Milton
memes.description: English poet and polemicist (1608–1674), author of *Paradise Lost*, referenced by Belloc for his poetic grandeur and as a living link to the literary past.

memes.label: Lighthouse
memes.description: Lighthouses as fixed points of guidance and symbols of human order along the wild coasts Belloc navigates in The Cruise of the Nona.

memes.label: little-muddipool
memes.description: A hypothetical place name deployed by Belloc as a satirical device to mock academic pedantry in matters of historical evidence.

memes.label: looe-stream
memes.description: Tidal stream off the Cornish coast, described by Belloc as the gateway into the Solent and a key navigational feature on the English Channel.

memes.label: Lord Haldane
memes.description: British statesman, philosopher, and army reformer whose pre-war work at the War Office made possible Britain's military cooperation with France in 1914 — singled out by Belloc as one of two men who deserved well of their country at the outbreak of the Great War.

memes.label: Lundy
memes.description: An island in the Bristol Channel, treated by Belloc as an exemplar of the small independent kingdom — a theme that runs through his reflections on islands and self-governance.

memes.label: Milford Haven
memes.description: A long fjord-like natural harbour in Pembrokeshire, Wales — a historic inlet where Henry Tudor landed and from which Cromwell's Irish invasion sailed, navigated by Belloc through fog and calm aboard the Nona.

memes.label: New Testament
memes.description: The second part of the Christian Bible, discussed in The Cruise of the Nona through Belloc's references to the Gospel of Saint John and the debate over its apostolic authorship.

memes.label: The Old Testament
memes.description: The Hebrew Scriptures, cited by Belloc in discussions of Protestant bibliolatry and the contrast between Catholic and Protestant approaches to scriptural authority.

memes.label: On Nothing
memes.description: A collection of essays by Hilaire Belloc himself, mentioned in the dedication of The Cruise of the Nona as one of his earlier books dedicated to Maurice Baring.

memes.label: Once Aboard the Lugger
memes.description: A novel by A. S. M. Hutchinson, praised by Belloc as amusing and fresh but overshadowed in sales by the author's later bestseller.

memes.label: organization-index
memes.description: Complete index of all organization entries in The Cruise of the Nona wiki, dynamically generated from page metadata.

memes.label: The Outline of History
memes.description: H. G. Wells's popular history of the world, discussed by Belloc as an example of a bestseller that confirmed readers' prejudices rather than offering permanent literary value.

memes.label: The Owers
memes.description: A group of submerged rocks and shoals off the Sussex coast near Selsey Bill, a navigational hazard that Belloc treats as a boundary marker between the sheltered waters of the Solent and the open Channel.

memes.label: padstow
memes.description: Cornish north-coast harbour town noted by Belloc as historically significant despite its shallow waters, part of the string of northern Cornish harbours that faced the main civilisation of the continent.

memes.label: person-index
memes.description: Complete index of all person entries in The Cruise of the Nona wiki, dynamically generated from page metadata.

memes.label: Pilot
memes.description: The figure of the pilot — both the harbour guides Belloc encounters and the pilot books he consults — as a mediator between the sailor and the hazards of unfamiliar coasts in The Cruise of the Nona.

memes.label: place-index
memes.description: Complete index of all place entries in The Cruise of the Nona wiki, dynamically generated from page metadata.

memes.label: poole
memes.description: Dorset harbour town with a large natural harbour, featured as a significant waypoint and departure point in Belloc's sailing narrative.

memes.label: Portland Harbour
memes.description: A large artificial harbour on the Dorset coast, built in the nineteenth century — described by Belloc as one of the very rare successes of man in his coaxing of the sea.

memes.label: Punch
memes.description: The British satirical weekly magazine, mentioned by Belloc alongside The Times as a pillar of English institutional culture.

memes.label: English Reformation
memes.description: The 16th-century religious transformation of England, referenced by Belloc as a historical watershed that destroyed popular monarchy and reshaped the relationship between the English people and their rulers.

memes.label: Selsea Bill
memes.description: A low headland on the West Sussex coast, marking the entry to the Looe Stream and serving as one of Belloc's familiar sea-marks on his home waters between Selsea and the Downs.

memes.label: Spanish Armada
memes.description: The 1588 Spanish naval expedition against England, referenced by Belloc in a single line alongside Sir Francis Drake as an example of the degraded modern mind's reliance on anecdotal history rather than structural understanding of the past.

memes.label: standard-pages
memes.description: Index of standard-quality wiki entries — solid, well-developed pages that form the content backbone of The Cruise of the Nona wiki.

memes.label: stephen-reynolds
memes.description: Writer and social reformer, close friend of Belloc, who sailed the Nona round Land's End — remembered by Belloc as a man too good for the modern world.

memes.label: T. H. Huxley
memes.description: English biologist and writer (1825–1895), known as "Darwin's Bulldog" for his defence of evolution, praised by Belloc for his intellectual courage and the quality of his prose.

memes.label: The Book of Job
memes.description: A book of the Old Testament, invoked by Belloc in The Cruise of the Nona as a model for his own method of asking questions without providing answers — "I, for my part, am with Job, and against the scientists."

memes.label: The Fool's Paradise
memes.description: A sixteenth-century work in the "literature of folly" tradition, mentioned by Belloc alongside The Praise of Folly and The Ship of Fools as an exemplar of a genre he wished to revive.

memes.label: The Green Elephant
memes.description: A play by Maurice Baring, mentioned by Hilaire Belloc in the dedication of The Cruise of the Nona as the counterpart to his own novel The Green Overcoat.

memes.label: The Green Overcoat
memes.description: A novel by Hilaire Belloc, dedicated to his friend Maurice Baring and mentioned in the dedication of The Cruise of the Nona as a token of their shared "communion in green things."

memes.label: The Odyssey
memes.description: Homer's epic poem, alluded to by Belloc as the archetype of seafaring literature whose spirit lives on in the Admiralty sailing directions.

memes.label: The Praise of Folly
memes.description: The satirical essay by Desiderius Erasmus (1511), mentioned by Belloc in The Cruise of the Nona alongside The Ship of Fools as an exemplar of the sixteenth-century literature of folly.

memes.label: The Sea
memes.description: "The central physical and metaphorical presence in Belloc's sailing memoir — at once a realm of adventure, a test of character, and a philosophical mirror."

memes.label: The Ship of Fools
memes.description: Sebastian Brant's Renaissance satire, cited by Belloc as one of the classic works on folly that he would like to see updated for modern times.

memes.label: The Spectator
memes.description: The British weekly magazine, referenced by Belloc in passing as a forum for academic dispute and absurdity.

memes.label: The Time Machine
memes.description: H. G. Wells's classic science-fiction novella, praised by Belloc as first-class literature likely to endure despite its author's later, more popular but lesser work.

memes.label: The Times
memes.description: The London daily newspaper, referenced by Belloc with characteristic irony about its supposed wisdom and its limitations as a source of real knowledge.

memes.label: Tide
memes.description: The rhythm of tidal streams and their central role in Belloc's coastal navigation — dictating every departure, passage, and landfall in The Cruise of the Nona.

memes.label: top-referenced
memes.description: Top 50 most cross-referenced wiki entries — the central hubs of the knowledge graph for The Cruise of the Nona.

memes.label: Trent's Last Case
memes.description: A detective novel by E. C. Bentley (1913), praised by Belloc in The Cruise of the Nona as "without a doubt, the best detective story in English today."

memes.label: vessel-index
memes.description: Complete index of all vessel entries in The Cruise of the Nona wiki — ships and boats mentioned in Belloc's sailing memoir.

memes.label: West Coast Pilot
memes.description: The Admiralty sailing directions for the western coasts of Britain, cited by Belloc alongside the Channel Pilot as essential nautical reference.

memes.label: William Pitt the Younger
memes.description: British statesman (1759–1806), the youngest Prime Minister in British history, referenced by Belloc in his analysis of eighteenth-century political corruption.

memes.label: William Wordsworth
memes.description: English Romantic poet (1770–1850), Poet Laureate, referenced by Belloc as the spokesman of modern pantheism and a case study in disproportionate literary reputation.

memes.label: Wisdom
memes.description: Belloc's treatment of wisdom as practical understanding gained through experience, especially from the sea

memes.label: work-index
memes.description: Complete index of all work entries in The Cruise of the Nona wiki, dynamically generated from page metadata.

memes.label: works-by-year
memes.description: Chronological index of literary and historical works referenced in The Cruise of the Nona, sorted by publication year.

memes.label: world-war-i
memes.description: The Great War (1914–1918), repeatedly discussed by Belloc as a pivotal historical event that exposed political and moral failures of his era.
